content.full_text:
MEMBERS
BILL FILANTE
Vice Chair
JERRY EAVES
Majority Vice Chair

CHIEF ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICER
BOB CONNELLY

California Legislature

COMMITTEE SECRETARY
KAYE PACKARD

Assembly Rules Committee

ROBERT FRAZEE
TRICE HARVEY
RICHARD MOUNTJOY
RICHARD POLANCO
LUCILLE ROYBAL-ALLARD
CURTIS R. TUCKER, JR.

ROOM 3016 — STATE CAPITOL
P.O. BOX 942849
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 94249-0001
TELEPHONE (916) 445-8173

TOM BANE
CHAIRMAN

December 18, 1991

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

The Assembly Rules Committee has been asked to whom members will
be able to send mailings once new legislative districts have been
formally adopted by the courts. Because this question may be
relevant to the mass mailing prohibition in the Political Reform
Act, I am requesting your advice.

It is my understanding that the change in district lines is
relevant to mailings sent pursuant to Regulation 18901(b)(9)(A),
which applies to mailings to a member's "constituents." Mailings
of 200 pieces or less, "letterhead" mailings pursuant to
Regulation 18901(b)(1), announcements of "official agency events"
sent pursuant to 18901(b)(9)(B), and mailings sent pursuant to
the other subdivisions of 18901 are not dependent on the
definition of the term "constituent."

My question is whether the residents of the districts as they
were defined in the 1990 elections, or the residents of the newly
drawn districts for the 1992 elections, or both, are a member's
"constituents" for purposes of 18901(b)(9)(A).

Printed on Recycled Paper

Scott Hallabrin
December 18, 1991
Page two


As we discussed, it is unclear to whom a voter will look for
representation in 1992. It is equally reasonable to expect
assistance from the member who represented your district in the
last election as from the member who represents the district as
it will be in 1992. In addition, if the term "constituent" is
restricted to the residents of the district at the time the
member was elected, there will be some individuals who will not
be constituents of any state senator between the 1992 and the
1994 elections. Therefore I would urge you to give serious
consideration to using both the old and the new district lines to
determine constituency, once a final determination has been made
as to the district boundaries for 1992.

We expect a final ruling on new district lines by the California
Supreme Court by the end of January, 1992. It would be very
helpful if we could get your response to our request before the
court decision.

Sincerely,

Barbara Milman
Chief Counsel, Assembly Rules Committee

BM:kp

It is equally reasonable to expect assistance from the member who represented your district in the last election as from the member who represents the district as it will be in 1992.
